coredata.dc:title: Can sonochemistry take place in the absence of cavitation? – A complementary view of how ultrasound can interact with materials
coredata.dc:creator: Vinatoru, Mircea
Mason, Timothy J.
coredata.dc:description: A hypothesis is advanced for a mechanism by which ultrasound could help to activate chemical reactions, even in the absence of cavitation. It is suggested that the compression phase of an acoustic wave could produce transient solid-like structures within the solution. These structures would result in an “ordering effect” of the molecules in which electrical charges could develop. Such electrical charges could facilitate electron movement from one molecule to another triggering therefore chemical reactions. Such reactions could occur even in the absence of cavitation especially if the solvent or reagents employed show piezoelectric/electrostriction properties. Similar transient ordering effects could be induced by the shockwave accompanying bubble collapse and these would help to explain some of the anomalous effects observed in sonochemistry under cavitation.

head.ce:title: Can sonochemistry take place in the absence of cavitation? – A complementary view of how ultrasound can interact with materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University POLITEHNICA of Bucharest, Faculty of Applied Chemistry and Materials Science, Spl. Independentei nr. 313, Sector 6, Bucharest RO-060042, Romania

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Applied Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A hypothesis is advanced for a mechanism by which ultrasound could help to activate chemical reactions, even in the absence of cavitation. It is suggested that the compression phase of an acoustic wave could produce transient solid-like structures within the solution. These structures would result in an “ordering effect” of the molecules in which electrical charges could develop. Such electrical charges could facilitate electron movement from one molecule to another triggering therefore chemical reactions. Such reactions could occur even in the absence of cavitation especially if the solvent or reagents employed show piezoelectric/electrostriction properties. Similar transient ordering effects could be induced by the shockwave accompanying bubble collapse and these would help to explain some of the anomalous effects observed in sonochemistry under cavitation.

ce:section.ce:para: Sonochemical effects occur at frequencies and wavelengths of ultrasound that are not of molecular dimensions but in spite of this these waves strongly interact with materials. There are many physical and chemical studies exploring such interactions mainly based on the results of acoustic cavitation [1,2]. Several theories have been advanced to explain the ways in which cavitation can influence chemical reactions and these include an electric discharge theory originally suggested in 1940 [3,4], the formation of a plasma in the collapsing bubble [5,6] but the most popular and widely accepted theory is the hot-spot theory which can be traced back to 1956 [7,8]. According to this the collapse of an acoustic cavitation bubble develops great intensities of pressure and temperature.

In order to create cavitation bubbles, it is necessary to reach an acoustic power high enough to pull apart the liquid molecules in the rarefaction phase, creating voids – the seeds of cavitation. This power is known as the cavitation threshold and its value for a particular system depends on the physical conditions and composition of the liquids used. Since laboratory ultrasonic sources operate above this threshold there is a generally accepted concept that sonochemical reactions can only take place when cavitation bubbles are present.

However, there are examples in the literature which are somewhat difficult to rationalise in terms of cavitation collapse alone and some of these were identified two years ago by Tim Mason in a paper published in this journal [9]. In his paper he referred to a presentation made by Mircea Vinatoru in 1994 entitled “Ordering effects in sonochemistry”. This presentation was part of a COST D6 Workshop “Fundamentals in Cavitation” associated with the 4th meeting of the European Society of Sonochemistry ESS4 held in Blankenberge, Belgium. In his presentation Mircea Vinatoru suggested that there might be other factors in addition to cavitation bubble collapse which could help in the interpretation of sonochemistry mechanisms. These involved the development of temporary solid-like structures induced within a liquid by the pressure effects of sonic waves. Such solid like structures could develop electrical charges especially if the solvent and/or the reagents involved have piezoelectric/electrostriction properties. Several examples exist of organic compounds with piezoelectric properties of which one of the first to be discovered was potassium sodium tartrate tetrahydrate, also known as Rochelle salt [10] but many other organic materials exhibit such properties e.g. polymers [11], liquid crystals [12] or biological tissues [13]. The pressure effects could be generated either during the compression phase of the sound wave itself or from the shock wave produced by cavitation bubble collapse.

Some three years after this presentation in which the pressure effects in sonochemistry were highlighted, Jean-Louis Luche commented on the subject in a paper entitled “A few questions on the sonochemistry of solutions” that: “The pressure effects of cavitation require investigation. In particular, the stereochemical changes observed in some reactions, even if infrequent, should be used to probe these effects” [14]. Some of these anomalous effects were listed in this paper and Luche also commented on the similarity of such reactions when performed under high pressures. In the early days of sonochemistry the role of cavitation in chemical reactions became a popular research topic [15,16]. Some unusual reactions were reported amongst which was the well-known “sonochemical switching of reaction pathways” [17].

There are very few studies which report any chemical or physical effects brought about by low power ultrasound below the cavitation threshold. The vast majority of applications of low power and generally high frequency ultrasound are not chemical but are in the area of non-destructive testing, medical scanning or particle separation using standing waves. Nevertheless, there are a few references to what might be termed anomalous effects of sub-cavitation ultrasound which can be ascribed to ordering effects. These and some of the more unusual results of cavitation chemistry are explored below.

It should be emphasised that the authors fully accept that the driving force in sonochemistry is predominantly acoustic cavitation. However, if an ordering effect exists it is to be expected that it will also be present to some extent in reactions driven by cavitation. Thus, our new mechanistic hypothesis is put forward as a means of explaining some anomalous sonochemical reactions that either occur in the absence of cavitation or that are difficult to explain in terms of cavitation alone.

Ultrasound can change the properties of a liquid by producing standing waves and the pressure differences generated by such standing waves can be used to separate particles suspended in that liquid. These studies date back to the 1930s when Sollner and Bondy at University College, London investigated the movement of particles suspended in a liquid when exposed to ultrasound [18]. They showed that quartz particles (density 2.66) in water collect at pressure antinodes, while toluene droplets (density 0.87) in water move to pressure nodes. Several years later in 1971 Mary Dyson at Guys’ Hospital Medical School, UK published a letter in Nature concerning the effect of diagnostic ultrasound on blood flow which stimulated much interest and activity [19]. She had observed that it was possible to stop the flow of red blood cells in veins in the presence of diagnostic ultrasound. In subsequent years the acoustic manipulation of particles has become an important study in its own right [20]. Nowadays ultrasonic technologies exist for the separation of both biological and nonbiological suspended particles and droplets based on their size and density. One method involves microfluidic technology employing 350 µm channels [21,22]. In the laboratory the separation of particles in water can be demonstrated in a very simple experiment where copper bronze, suspended in water is placed in a measuring cylinder and immersed in a 40 kHz ultrasonic cleaning bath [23]. Under these conditions, using a cleaning bath as the source of ultrasound, it is almost certain that some cavitation will be present but despite this a separation of particles into bands occurs. We suggest that the pressure effect generated in the compression phase of an acoustic wave may also force not only particles but also molecules together as in the case of a standing wave field.

However, the effect of such forces on the alignment of molecules rather than separation of particles is rather less well known. We have called this an “ordering effect” and it has been demonstrated for some liquids and solutions which, when exposed to ultrasound become birefringent (doubly refracting) [24]. Ultrasound can also influence the orientation of nematic liquid crystals (NLC). In the absence of external action, a normally oriented NLC layer placed between crossed polaroids is opaque to the light wave that illuminates the layer along the crystal axis. The effect of an acoustic field is to tilt the crystal molecules in a way which makes the layer transparent [25]. The light transmission can be controlled by modulating the ultrasonic driving frequency and voltage. In 2007 Matsuoka concluded that ultrasonically induced birefringence was a useful method to for the investigation of orientational dynamics in colloidal solutions [26].

We suggest that during the compression phase of an acoustic wave it might be possible to develop, if only for a very short time, solid-like structures within the liquid as the molecules are forced together. In this situation there is the possibility that transient piezoelectric properties could develop either in the compressed liquid or in substances dissolved in that liquid. As a consequence of this and for a short period the transient quasi-solid phase would develop charges on its surface. A very similar situation could arise as a result of shock waves generated by collapsing bubbles which are also capable of creating strongly compressed regions in a liquid. Within such charged structures electron mobility will increase and this could induce chemical reactions, via well accepted electron transfer mechanisms [27–29]. The evidence for this is sparse but nevertheless some reported experimental observations appear to lend support.

An interesting phenomenon discovered over 60 years ago is related to the ultrasonically induced electrical potential, called “U-effect I” and “U-effect II”. When an ultrasonic vibration is passed through a capillary containing an electrolyte, an electric potential is developed at both ends of the capillary. This was called “U-effect I” [30–32]. A similar vibrating potential was observed at the interface between mercury and an electrolyte, when an ultrasonic wave passed through [33]. This effect should not be confused with the ultrasonic capillary effect (UCE) which was first reported in the 1960′s and is a dramatic increase of the depth and velocity of penetration of a liquid into a capillary channel during sonication. Dezhkunov suggested that the most likely mechanism involves cavitation [34]. It was shown that with small gaps between the capillary and the emitting face of the ultrasonic transducer the thresholds for both the initiation of cavitation and for capillary rise virtually coincided. Increasing the amplitude to increase sonoluminescence also increased the ultrasonic capillary effect [35]. Whether there is any contribution from an ordering effect in UCE is not clear.

When an organic liquid such as hexane or toluene is placed in an ultrasonic field, a marked change in electrical conductivity is induced but when the sonication is stopped the conductivity returns to its initial value. When the acoustic field is removed the conductance quite rapidly returns to its original value. This behaviour was first interpreted by Meyer as a transfer of adsorbed impurities from the electrodes into the liquid [36]. A few years later Seidl investigated the change in conductivity of toluene, hexane, and carbon tetrachloride and proposed an alternative explanation involving the transient formation of “complex ions” and a consequent increase in either the number or in the mobility of the charge carriers [37]. The latter view was supported by Berkowitz who also commented that it might involve “a much more complex – and not as yet fully understood behaviour pattern” [38]. Ultrasound at a frequency of 1.25 MHz below cavitation threshold increases the electrical conductivity of benzene and halogenated benzenes during sonolysis [39]. Similar effects have been reported in the electric conductivity of n-butyl alcohol and tert-butyl alcohol [40]. IR and NMR spectroscopy revealed that no appreciable chemical changes were brought about by sonication. The same group examined the effect of ultrasound on the breakdown voltage of n-butyl alcohol, cyclohexane and transformer oil [41]. The voltage drops for the three were 20 to ∼8 kV, ∼13 kV to ∼7 kV and 18–11 kV in 15 min respectively during irradiation and each returned to its initial value when the ultrasound was turned off. A rather unsatisfying conclusion was drawn that it was either the direct or the indirect effect on cavitation that may be the cause of the decrease in breakdown voltage.

A change in conductivity with sonication has been demonstrated in the case of highly purified nitrobenzene (Fig. 1 ) [42]. We believe that the reported measurements of nitrobenzene (NB) conductivity can be used to help to justify our hypothesis. The conductivity changes were only measured when the ultrasound was turned off (to avoid deterioration of conductivity cell through ultrasonic removal of the black platinum coating the electrodes) [42].
Over a sonication period of 1 h (indirect sonication in a Langford T175 ultrasonic cleaning bath 40 kHz) the initial conductance value of 0.001 µS reached 16 µS.

The explanation that was suggested at that time was that nitrobenzene, under ultrasonic irradiation, could be split (via ultrasonic outer space electron transfer) into a pair of ion radicals [43] (as postulated by Seidl in 1940 as complex ions [37]): Support for this unusual behaviour of nitrobenzene is provided by reports of its chemical properties. According to Vogel “Nitrobenzene is an extremely versatile solvent, and may frequently be employed for the crystallisation of compounds which do not dissolve appreciably in the common organic solvents.” [44]. However he goes on to say that “The only disadvantage of nitrobenzene as a solvent is that it has a pronounced oxidising action at the boiling point.” In our view “pronounced oxidising action at the boiling point” indicates that a molecule of nitrobenzene could give oxygen to another molecule. But nitrobenzene is a very stable molecule and so we suggest that the only way that this could occur is via electron transfer:

From such an ion radical pair it is easy to imagine the initiation of chemical reactions. While the anion radical of nitrobenzene is relatively stable, the radical cation is less stable and suffers rapid decomposition to phenyl radical and NO2 + or NO2 radical and phenyl cation (the latter is less probable), leading to a series of compounds identifiable by GC–MS analyses [45,46].

It is not unreasonable to think that water could follow a similar pathway when sonicated. This would be facilitated by the multitude of hydrogen bonds throughout the structure which would enable electrons to travel (inner sphere transfer) within the water structure:

Solvent properties play an important role in creating conditions under which ultrasound can generate transient charged solid-like structures. As mentioned above those solvents with some form of piezoelectric properties are more likely to produce such structures and could then facilitate chemical reactions. One example is water (in the form of ice) which shows an electrification effect by rubbing [47]. Ionic liquids would be also expected to promote chemical reactions by this route when sonicated. Suspended solid materials like catalysts, could act as charged centres during sonication. The solid-like structures would have a short life time, τ, which could be a function F of ultrasonic frequency (f), reaction temperature (T), external pressure (P), molecule polarizability (α) and a solvent rearrangement factor (the availability of the liquid to return to initial state) (λ):
1
τ = F
f , T , P , α , λ

If such a solid like structure can exist, even for a very short time, it seems possible that this may have the effect (under appropriate temperature and saturation conditions) of inducing crystallization. Effectively this could be the initial seeding effect leading to the well-known use of ultrasound to induce crystallisation [48–50].

There would be other consequences that follow from the formation of transient solid-like structures within sonicated liquids including the effect of electrostatic repulsion between similar charges which would create easier conditions for the onset of cavitation. For some reaction mixtures or liquids, with greater potential for piezoelectric properties, the cavitation threshold could be dramatically lowered. Also, those compounds showing piezoelectric properties will be more susceptible to the effect of sonication (sugars for example). Solvents or compounds which are able to exchange electrons or have potential electrostriction properties e.g. ionic liquids will promote sonochemical reactions. Water at lower temperatures would also be a suitable solvent for sonochemical reactions. It is also reasonable to think that due to the solid-like structure in the compression phase stereo selective reactions would be possible.

Under the conditions outlined above the authors believe that it is not always necessary to reach the cavitation threshold to induce sonochemical reactions – in other words sonochemical reactions could occur in the absence of cavitation. This hypothesis brings with it the need for a new range of experimental sonochemistry targeted at shedding light on the idea of an “ordering effect”. Naturally we would be happy to see some corroboration of our ideas but, as true scientists, we would also be prepared to accept results which disprove it.

If the above ideas prove to have some substance, then the addition of an ordering effect to current theories of the mechanisms that drive sonochemistry should lead to more predictable outcomes of sonochemical reactions or processes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: One of the authors (MV) acknowledge the financial support received from the Competitiveness Operational Programme 2014-2020, Action 1.1.4: Attracting high-level personnel from abroad in order to enhance the RD capacity, project: P_37_471, “Ultrasonic/Microwave Nonconventional Techniques as new tools for nonchemical and chemical processes”, financed by contract: 47/05.09.2016.
